tech.summary: Chile’s CNE has released preliminary terms for the 2026/01 power supply tender. This technology-neutral process favors renewables by excluding high-emission fuels and allowing storage-backed bids. Utilizing 15-year contracts and time-of-day demand blocks, the structure is designed to support photovoltaic and hybrid solar-plus-storage projects, specifically targeting daytime and evening peak coverage requirements.

tech.headline: SNEC 2026 shows solar shifting toward system integration and AI
tech.summary: SNEC 2026 highlighted a significant industry shift from module-centric growth toward system integration. Storage and AI are becoming increasingly central to project design and value creation. At the technology level, back-contact technology gained momentum as a premium platform, while emerging applications and scenario-specific modules signaled a trend toward deeper market segmentation within the solar sector.

tech.headline: Longi develops 27.27% efficient heterojunction back-contact solar cell
tech.summary: Longi and Yangzhou University researchers developed a laser-based manufacturing strategy for HJT back-contact solar cells. This method mitigates shock-wave-induced damage, improving passivation stability and device performance. The approach resulted in a certified 27.27% efficient solar cell, providing an industrially viable pathway toward the fabrication of ultra-high-efficiency photovoltaic technology through improved laser processing techniques.

tech.headline: Perovskite Tandem Solar Cells Show Degradation in Outdoor Tests
tech.summary: One-year outdoor testing by TNO and Fraunhofer ISE revealed efficiency drops in perovskite/perovskite/silicon triple-junction cells from 17–18% to 13–14%. Key issues included voltage loss, encapsulation delamination, and UV/thermal-induced layer degradation, not absorber instability.

learn.title: Agrivoltaics: Farming Under Solar Panels
learn.intro: Combining solar arrays with crops lets farmers earn electricity revenue while protecting plants from extreme weather. This dual‑use approach boosts land productivity, cuts water use, and expands clean energy without needing extra acreage—key for feeding a growing population and meeting climate goals.
learn.c1_text: Solar panels provide 30‑50% shade, lowering field temperatures by up to 5 °C. Like a canopy that cools a garden, this reduces evapotranspiration, saving up to 30% water—crucial in arid regions such as Rajasthan.
learn.c2_text: A 1‑MW agrivoltaic system can generate ~1,500 MWh annually while supporting 2‑3 ha of crops. The electricity offsets grid emissions, and the farmer receives power purchase payments, creating a steady income stream beyond harvest sales.
learn.c3_text: In Gujarat, a 2‑MW agrivoltaic farm produces 2,800 MWh and grows leafy greens with 25% higher yields. The state’s Solar Policy 2022 offers 30% capital subsidy for such dual‑use projects, accelerating adoption.
learn.stat1: India: 12 GW agrivoltaic capacity under study (2024)
learn.stat2: Global agrivoltaic land use: 4 GW total (2023)
learn.stat3: Water savings: up to 30% on shaded fields
learn.stat4: Crop yield boost: 10‑25% reported in pilot sites

learn.title: How Lithium‑Ion Batteries Power the Grid
learn.intro: Lithium‑ion batteries store excess renewable power and release it when demand spikes, smoothing variability. Understanding their operation helps planners deploy cost‑effective storage that keeps lights on and cuts reliance on fossil peaker plants.
learn.c1_text: A lithium‑ion cell moves Li⁺ ions from a graphite anode to a lithium‑metal oxide cathode during discharge, like marbles rolling from one bowl to another, releasing electrons that flow as electricity.
learn.c2_text: Modules are linked into racks, then into megawatt‑hour storage plants. A 100 MW/400 MWh system can shift 4 hours of solar peak output, reducing curtailment by up to 15% in high‑solar regions.
learn.c3_text: The Ministry of Power’s 2023 scheme targets 5 GW of lithium‑ion storage by 2030. Projects in Tamil Nadu pair 50 MW batteries with solar farms, cutting diesel generator use by 20,000 hours annually.
learn.stat4: India: 2 GW battery capacity under construction (2024)

learn.title: Green Bonds Finance Renewable Projects
learn.intro: Green bonds channel private capital into clean‑energy projects, offering investors a way to support climate goals while earning returns. They are a cornerstone of the financing toolkit needed to meet global net‑zero targets.
learn.c1_text: A bond whose proceeds are earmarked for environmentally beneficial projects—like a loan that can only be spent on solar farms or wind turbines. Investors receive regular interest, and issuers report on the climate impact.
learn.c2_text: Issuers use the Green Bond Principles to disclose project selection, expected emissions avoided, and third‑party verification. Typical metrics: megawatts installed or tonnes CO₂e avoided per year.
learn.c3_text: In 2023, India issued $1.2 bn of sovereign green bonds, financing 5 GW of solar capacity and 2 GW of wind. The RBI’s green bond framework encourages banks to hold a minimum 5% of portfolios in such securities.
learn.stat1: Global green bond issuance: $550 bn in 2023
learn.stat3: India: 12 GW renewable projects funded via bonds (2024)
learn.stat4: CO₂ avoided by bond‑financed projects: 150 Mt yr⁻¹

learn.title: Green Hydrogen: PEM vs Alkaline Electrolysis
learn.intro: Electrolyzers split water into hydrogen and oxygen using renewable electricity. Two leading technologies—Proton Exchange Membrane (PEM) and alkaline—offer different performance, cost, and scalability profiles, shaping how quickly green hydrogen can scale.
learn.c1_text: PEM units use a solid polymer membrane to conduct protons, operating at 50‑80 °C. They can ramp up to 100% load in seconds, similar to a car’s electric motor, making them ideal for variable renewable input.
learn.c2_text: Alkaline cells employ a liquid KOH electrolyte at 70‑90 °C. They are cheaper (≈$400/kW) but slower to start and less tolerant of rapid power swings, akin to a diesel engine that needs warm‑up time.
learn.c3_text: India’s National Hydrogen Mission plans 10 GW of electrolyzers by 2030, with 60% PEM for offshore wind integration in Gujarat and 40% alkaline for large‑scale fertilizer hubs in Madhya Pradesh.
learn.stat4: India: 1.5 GW green hydrogen projects under pipeline (2024)

learn.title: Pumped Hydro: The World’s Largest Battery
learn.intro: Pumped hydro stores energy by moving water uphill when electricity is cheap and releasing it to generate power when demand peaks. It provides gigawatt‑scale, multi‑hour storage that balances grids worldwide.
learn.c1_text: During low demand, surplus power runs pumps that lift water to an upper reservoir—think of filling a bathtub uphill. When electricity is needed, water flows back down through turbines, generating power instantly.
learn.c2_text: A typical 1 GW pumped‑hydro plant can store 4‑8 GWh, enough to power 1 million homes for 4 hours. Efficiency is 70‑80%, comparable to a rechargeable battery but at much larger scale.
learn.c3_text: India’s Ministry of Power estimates 30 GW of pumped‑hydro potential, especially in the Himalayas. Projects in Himachal Pradesh aim for 1.5 GW/6 GWh, supporting the country’s 450 GW renewable target for 2030.
learn.stat3: Cost of storage: $30‑$50 per kWh installed
learn.stat4: India: 2 GW pumped hydro under construction (2024)

learn.title: Second‑Life EV Batteries for Grid Storage
learn.intro: After reaching 80% capacity, EV batteries still hold enough energy for stationary use. Repurposing them extends their life, reduces waste, and provides cost‑effective storage for renewables.
learn.c1_text: A 60 kWh EV pack, once retired, can deliver 40‑45 kWh at stationary use—similar to a used laptop battery still powering a home office for years. This second life can last 8‑10 more years in a stationary setting.
learn.c2_text: Second‑life projects cut storage costs by 30‑40% versus new batteries. Revenue streams include frequency regulation, peak shaving, and backup power, making the investment attractive for utilities.
learn.c3_text: In Delhi, a pilot uses 200 retired Tata Nexon batteries for a 5 MWh community microgrid, reducing diesel generator run‑time by 25%. The Ministry of New & Renewable Energy offers subsidies for such repurposing schemes.
learn.stat2: Cost per kWh: $80‑$120 for repurposed packs
learn.stat3: Lifecycle CO₂ savings: 30 t per 100 kWh reused
